description: Transitive-propagation vectors for SemVer-Trust (spec/semver-trust.md §5.3). Each vector carries an internal dependency graph — nodes with per-component own trust, directed edges [consumer, dependency] — and asserts the effective trust of every node: effective(C) = min(own(C), min over deps D of effective(D)), with dependency cycles collapsed to their strongly connected component. Where 'floor_source' is asserted it names, per node, the component whose own trust set the floor (the node itself when its own trust attains the minimum).

vectors.description: Without dependency edges, effective trust equals own trust: propagation flows only along the graph.

Appendix A step 2: auth's five human-reviewed commits give own(auth) = T3, but auth depends on common at own(common) = T0, so effective(auth) = min(T3, T0) = T0 with common as the floor source. (billing's own trust is unstated in Appendix A steps 1-4 and is omitted here; the full auth/billing/common fan-out shape is exercised by propagation/shared-dependency-fanout.)

Appendix A steps 3-4: a post-hoc review attestation lifts own(common) to T2; the cascade re-evaluation gives effective(auth) = min(own T3, common T2) = T2. Same graph, new evidence, no source rebuild.

The Appendix A workspace shape with both services present: one under-trusted shared library floors every consumer — shared packages are where human review buys the most trust, because their level multiplies across every consumer.
The floor propagates transitively, not just across direct edges: a T1 leaf floors a consumer two hops away.
Diamond graph: the consumer's floor is the minimum across both branches; the base does not inherit its consumers' levels (propagation follows dependency direction only).
When a component's own trust is already the minimum, its dependencies do not lower it further and the floor source is the component itself.
A dependency cycle collapses to its strongly connected component: every member of the SCC shares the SCC's minimum own trust, regardless of position in the cycle.
An SCC floors its consumers like any other dependency: a component outside the cycle that depends on any member inherits the SCC minimum; an upstream dependency of the cycle is unaffected.
Two cycles connected by a one-way edge remain distinct SCCs: the downstream cycle's floor propagates into the upstream one, not the reverse.
